paragraphs.text:
%md
# 🎬 STQD6324 Data Management — Assignment 2
### MovieLens 100K Data Pipeline: <font color='teal'>Apache Spark</font> + <font color='navy'>Cassandra</font>

---

### ⚙️ Pipeline Overview
| Step | Description |
|------|-------------|
| 1 | Environment Configuration |
| 2 | Download MovieLens dataset |
| 3 | Load Data into HDFS  |
| 4 | Parse Raw Data into RDDs |
| 5 | Transform RDDs into Spark DataFrames |
| 6 | Data cleaning & preprocessing |
| 7 | Analytical queries using Spark SQL |
| 8 | Write results to Cassandra |
| 9 | Read back from Cassandra for validation |
| 10 | Session Completion |

msg.data:
🎬 STQD6324 Data Management — Assignment 2

MovieLens 100K Data Pipeline: Apache Spark + Cassandra

⚙️ Pipeline Overview

| Step | Description |
|——|————-|
| 1 | Environment Configuration |
| 2 | Download MovieLens dataset |
| 3 | Load Data into HDFS |
| 4 | Parse Raw Data into RDDs |
| 5 | Transform RDDs into Spark DataFrames |
| 6 | Data cleaning & preprocessing |
| 7 | Analytical queries using Spark SQL |
| 8 | Write results to Cassandra |
| 9 | Read back from Cassandra for validation |
| 10 | Session Completion |

paragraphs.text:
%md
## 1. Environment Configuration

This pipeline was executed in a pre-configured Hadoop-based distributed cluster environment (**Hortonworks HDP stack**).

---

>  🛠️ System Components(Observed at Runtime)

 **Apache Zeppelin**: 0.7.3
 
 **Apache Spark**: 2.3.1 (HDP distribution)
 **PySpark**: 2.3.1 
 
 **Apache Cassandra**: 3.11.17 
 **CQLSH**: 5.0.1 (command-line interface used to interact with Cassandra)
 
 **Hadoop (HDFS)**: HDP Sandbox 
 **Python**: 2.7.5 


*Note: This environment is pre-configured and does not support manual upgrades of core system libraries.*

paragraphs.text:
%md
## 2. Download MovieLens dataset

The MovieLens 100K dataset is downloaded into a controlled working directory within the Zeppelin environment.  

---

> 📂 **Dataset Components**
- `u.data` — User ratings (user id, movie id, rating, timestamp) 
- `u.user` — User demographic information (user id, age, occupation, gender, zip code)
- `u.item` — Movie metadata (movie id, movie title, release date, video release date, IMDb URL, 19 genres )

---

*Note: This step ensures all required raw data files are consistently available before loading into HDFS for distributed processing using Apache Spark.*

msg.data:
2. Download MovieLens dataset

The MovieLens 100K dataset is downloaded into a controlled working directory within the Zeppelin environment.

📂 Dataset Components

  • u.data — User ratings (user id, movie id, rating, timestamp)
  • u.user — User demographic information (user id, age, occupation, gender, zip code)
  • u.item — Movie metadata (movie id, movie title, release date, video release date, IMDb URL, 19 genres )

Note: This step ensures all required raw data files are consistently available before loading into HDFS for distributed processing using Apache Spark.

paragraphs.text:
%sh

# Create a working directory
mkdir -p ml-100k-data

# Download dataset files
wget -O ml-100k-data/u.data http://media.sundog-soft.com/hadoop/ml-100k/u.data
wget -O ml-100k-data/u.user http://media.sundog-soft.com/hadoop/ml-100k/u.user
wget -O ml-100k-data/u.item http://media.sundog-soft.com/hadoop/ml-100k/u.item

echo "Downloaded!"

paragraphs.text:
%md
## 3. Load Data into HDFS

The MovieLens 100K dataset is stored in HDFS to enable distributed processing using Apache Spark. 
All files are uploaded into a dedicated HDFS directory.

---

> 📌 HDFS Target Directory
>`/user/ml-100k`


*Note: This step gets data into HDFS so Apache Spark can process it across the cluster, so that it is reliable and easy to access.*

msg.data:
3. Load Data into HDFS

The MovieLens 100K dataset is stored in HDFS to enable distributed processing using Apache Spark.
All files are uploaded into a dedicated HDFS directory.

📌 HDFS Target Directory
/user/ml-100k

Note: This step gets data into HDFS so Apache Spark can process it across the cluster, so that it is reliable and easy to access.

paragraphs.text:
%sh

# Create HDFS directory
hdfs dfs -mkdir -p /user/ml-100k

# Upload data
hdfs dfs -put -f ml-100k-data/u.data /user/ml-100k/
hdfs dfs -put -f ml-100k-data/u.user /user/ml-100k/
hdfs dfs -put -f ml-100k-data/u.item /user/ml-100k/

echo "Uploaded!"

msg.data: Uploaded!

paragraphs.text:
%sh

# Verify HDFS upload
echo "HDFS directory contents:"
hdfs dfs -ls /user/ml-100k

paragraphs.text:
%md
## 4. Parse Raw Data into RDDs

The raw datasets stored in HDFS are loaded into Spark **Resilient Distributed Datasets (RDDs)**. 

---

> 📂 RDD Objects
- `data_rdd` – User ratings dataset from `u.data`
- `user_rdd` – User demographic dataset from `u.user`
- `item_rdd` – Movie metadata dataset from `u.item`

*Note: Each dataset is initially read as raw text from HDFS and then parsed into structured RDDs using delimiter-based splitting.*

msg.data:
4. Parse Raw Data into RDDs

The raw datasets stored in HDFS are loaded into Spark Resilient Distributed Datasets (RDDs).

📂 RDD Objects

  • data_rdd – User ratings dataset from u.data
  • user_rdd – User demographic dataset from u.user
  • item_rdd – Movie metadata dataset from u.item

Note: Each dataset is initially read as raw text from HDFS and then parsed into structured RDDs using delimiter-based splitting.

paragraphs.text:
%md

#### 4.1 Load Raw RDDs

#####The raw dataset are loaded from HDFS as text based RDDs.

msg.data:
4.1 Load Raw RDDs

The raw dataset are loaded from HDFS as text based RDDs.

paragraphs.text:
%pyspark

# Load RDD from HDFS
data_rdd = sc.textFile("hdfs:///user/ml-100k/u.data")
user_rdd = sc.textFile("hdfs:///user/ml-100k/u.user")
item_rdd = sc.textFile("hdfs:///user/ml-100k/u.item")

# Checking
print("u.data sample:", data_rdd.take(3))
print("u.user sample:", user_rdd.take(3))
print("u.item sample:", item_rdd.take(3))

%md

#### 4.2 Parse and Transform RDDs

##### The raw text records are parsed into structured tuples by splitting each record using the appropriate delimiter. **Only the attributes required for subsequent analysis are retained.**

msg.data:
4.2 Parse and Transform RDDs

The raw text records are parsed into structured tuples by splitting each record using the appropriate delimiter. Only the attributes required for subsequent analysis are retained.

paragraphs.text:
%pyspark

# Parse u.data_rdd
ratings_rdd = data_rdd.map(lambda x:x.split("\t")) \
    .map(lambda x: (int(x[0]), int(x[1]), float(x[2])))

# Parse u.user_rdd
users_rdd = user_rdd.map(lambda x: x.split("|")) \
     .map(lambda x: (int(x[0]), int(x[1]), str(x[2]), str(x[3])))

# Parse u.item_rdd
movies_rdd = item_rdd.map(lambda x: x.split("|")) \
     .map(lambda x: (int(x[0]), str(x[1].encode('ascii', 'ignore').decode('ascii')), int(x[5]), int(x[6]), int(x[7]), int(x[8]), int(x[9]), int(x[10]), int(x[11]),
     int(x[12]), int(x[13]), int(x[14]), int(x[15]), int(x[16]), int(x[17]), int(x[18]), int(x[19]), int(x[20]), int(x[21]), int(x[22]), int(x[23])))
    
    
# Verify parsing
print("ratings_rdd:", ratings_rdd.take(3))
print("users_rdd:", users_rdd.take(3))
print("movies_rdd:", movies_rdd.take(3))

%md

## 5. Transform RDDs into Spark DataFrames

The parsed RDDs are converted into Spark DataFrames with defined schemas. 

---

> 📊 DataFrames Created
- `ratings_df` 
- `users_df` 
- `movies_df`


*Note: Defining schemas improves data consistency and enables SQL-based analytical queries in subsequent stages.*

msg.data:
5. Transform RDDs into Spark DataFrames

The parsed RDDs are converted into Spark DataFrames with defined schemas.

📊 DataFrames Created

  • ratings_df
  • users_df
  • movies_df

Note: Defining schemas improves data consistency and enables SQL-based analytical queries in subsequent stages.

paragraphs.text:
%pyspark

# Ratings DataFrame
ratings_df = spark.createDataFrame(ratings_rdd, ["user_id", "movie_id", "rating"])

# Users DataFrame
users_df = spark.createDataFrame(users_rdd, ["user_id", "age", "gender", "occupation"])

# Movies DataFrame
movies_df = spark.createDataFrame(movies_rdd, ["movie_id", "movie_title", "unknown","action","adventure","animation", "childrens","comedy","crime","documentary",
                                                "drama","fantasy","film_noir","horror", "musical","mystery","romance","sci_fi", "thriller","war","western"])


# View all DataFrames
# Ratings DataFrame
print("Preview of ratings_df:")
ratings_df.show(5)

# Users DataFrame
print("Preview of users_df:")
users_df.show(5)

# Movies DataFrame
print("Preview of movies_df:")
movies_df.show(5)

%md

## 6. Data Cleaning & Preprocessing
The DataFrames are inspected to ensure data quality before analytical processing.

---

> 🔍 **Checks Performed**
- Null value detection   
- Duplicatedetection   
- Structural Integrity Validation

*Note: Data cleaning is essential to ensure reliable analytical results in subsequent Spark SQL queries.*

msg.data:
6. Data Cleaning & Preprocessing

The DataFrames are inspected to ensure data quality before analytical processing.

🔍 Checks Performed

  • Null value detection
  • Duplicatedetection
  • Structural Integrity Validation

Note: Data cleaning is essential to ensure reliable analytical results in subsequent Spark SQL queries.

paragraphs.text:
%md

#### 6.1 Null Value Detection

##### Null value checks were performed across all datasets using Spark aggregation functions.


> ##### **Result:** No null values were detected in the `ratings`, `users`, and `movies` datasets.

msg.data:
6.1 Null Value Detection

Null value checks were performed across all datasets using Spark aggregation functions.
Result: No null values were detected in the ratings, users, and movies datasets.

paragraphs.text:
%pyspark

from pyspark.sql import functions as F

print("Null Value Check:")

# Null check in ratings_df
print("Ratings DataFrame")
ratings_df.select([
    F.sum(F.when(F.col(c).isNull(), 1).otherwise(0)).alias(c)
    for c in ratings_df.columns
]).show()

# Null check in users_df
print("Users DataFrame")
users_df.select([
    F.sum(F.when(F.col(c).isNull(), 1).otherwise(0)).alias(c)
    for c in users_df.columns
]).show()

# Null check in movies_df
print("Movies DataFrame")
movies_df.select([
    F.sum(F.when(F.col(c).isNull(), 1).otherwise(0)).alias(c)
    for c in movies_df.columns
]).show()

%md

#### 6.2 Duplicate Detection

##### Duplicate records were evaluated using Spark’s `dropDuplicates()` function*

> ##### **Result:** No duplicate rows  were observed. Row counts **remained identical** before and after the dropping process.

msg.data:
6.2 Duplicate Detection

Duplicate records were evaluated using Spark’s dropDuplicates() function*
Result: No duplicate rows were observed. Row counts remained identical before and after the dropping process.

paragraphs.text:
%pyspark

# Create function to remove duplicates
def get_cleaning_summary(df, name):
    
    # Count rows before cleaning
    before = df.count()
    
    # Remove duplicate rows
    df_clean = df.dropDuplicates()
    
    # Count rows after cleaning
    after = df_clean.count()
    
    # Return summary on cleaned DataFrame
    return Row(
        dataset=name,
        before_count=before,
        after_count=after,
        duplicates_removed=before - after
    ), df_clean


# Apply the function to each dataset
ratings_summary, ratings_df = get_cleaning_summary(ratings_df, "ratings")
users_summary, users_df = get_cleaning_summary(users_df, "users")
movies_summary, movies_df = get_cleaning_summary(movies_df, "movies")


# Combine all summaries into a single DataFrame 
summary_df = spark.createDataFrame([ratings_summary, users_summary, movies_summary])
summary_df.show()

%md

#### 6.3 Structural Integrity Validation

##### Schema validation and key uniqueness checks were performed to ensure datasets are suitable for join operations and analytical processing.

> ##### Primary key assumptions:
> ##### - `ratings_df`: (user_id, movie_id)
> #####- `users_df`: user_id
> ##### - `movies_df`: movie_id

paragraphs.text:
%md

## 7. Analytical queries using Spark SQL 

A series of analytical queries were executed using Spark SQL to extract meaningful insights from the MovieLens dataset.

---

> ### ✨Analytical Tasks

>- **Task 1**: Average rating for each movie  
>- **Task 2**: Top 10 movies by average rating
>- **Task 3**: Favourite genre of users who rated ≥ 50 movies
>- **Task 4**: All users under 20 years old
>- **Task 5**: Scientists aged between 30 and 40

*Note: Before executing these analytical tasks, the corresponding Spark DataFrames must be registered as SQL Temporary Views (`createOrReplaceTempView`) to enable standard SQL querying syntax.*

msg.data:
7. Analytical queries using Spark SQL

A series of analytical queries were executed using Spark SQL to extract meaningful insights from the MovieLens dataset.

✨Analytical Tasks

  • Task 1: Average rating for each movie
  • Task 2: Top 10 movies by average rating
  • Task 3: Favourite genre of users who rated ≥ 50 movies
  • Task 4: All users under 20 years old
  • Task 5: Scientists aged between 30 and 40

Note: Before executing these analytical tasks, the corresponding Spark DataFrames must be registered as SQL Temporary Views (createOrReplaceTempView) to enable standard SQL querying syntax.

paragraphs.text:
%pyspark

ratings_df.createOrReplaceTempView("ratings")
users_df.createOrReplaceTempView("users")
movies_df.createOrReplaceTempView("movies")

print("Temporary SQL views created successfully.")

msg.data: Temporary SQL views created successfully.

paragraphs.text:
%md

#### <font color='teal'>Task 1</font> — Average Rating for Each Movie
##### Calculate the average rating for every movie - by joining `ratings` with `movies`.

---

##### The output of this query can be visualized using Apache Zeppelin’s built-in interactive chart feature.

**Chart Setting:**
* **Keys:** `movie_title`
* **Values:** `avg_rating SUM`

msg.data:
Task 1 — Average Rating for Each Movie

Calculate the average rating for every movie - by joining ratings with movies.
The output of this query can be visualized using Apache Zeppelin’s built-in interactive chart feature.

Chart Setting:

  • Keys: movie_title
  • Values: avg_rating SUM

paragraphs.text:
%sql

SELECT
    r.movie_id,
    m.movie_title,
    ROUND(AVG(r.rating), 2) AS avg_rating,
    COUNT(*) AS num_ratings
FROM ratings r
JOIN movies m
ON r.movie_id = m.movie_id
GROUP BY r.movie_id, m.movie_title
LIMIT 10

paragraphs.text:
%md

#### 💬 Discussion — Task 1

> ##### This query computes the **average rating** and total number of ratings for each movie by joining the `ratings` and `movies` datasets.
> ##### The output provides a general overview of how users have rated different movies across the dataset. However, the results are not ordered and the displayed output does not represent a ranked list of movies. The ordering of rows is determined by Spark’s internal processing and should not be interpreted as meaningful ranking or priority.
> ##### A key observation is that some movies have a very low number of ratings. These averages are less reliable as a small sample size can heavily distort the data and lead to misleading conclusions.

msg.data:
💬 Discussion — Task 1

This query computes the average rating and total number of ratings for each movie by joining the ratings and movies datasets.
The output provides a general overview of how users have rated different movies across the dataset. However, the results are not ordered and the displayed output does not represent a ranked list of movies. The ordering of rows is determined by Spark’s internal processing and should not be interpreted as meaningful ranking or priority.
A key observation is that some movies have a very low number of ratings. These averages are less reliable as a small sample size can heavily distort the data and lead to misleading conclusions.

paragraphs.text:
%md

#### <font color='teal'>Task 2</font> — Top 10 Movies by Average Rating
##### Identify the top ten movies with the highest average ratings

---

##### The output of this query can be visualized using Apache Zeppelin’s built-in interactive chart feature.

**Chart Setting:**
* **Keys:** `movie_title`
* **Values:** `avg_rating SUM`

msg.data:
Task 2 — Top 10 Movies by Average Rating

Identify the top ten movies with the highest average ratings
The output of this query can be visualized using Apache Zeppelin’s built-in interactive chart feature.

Chart Setting:

  • Keys: movie_title
  • Values: avg_rating SUM

paragraphs.text:
%sql

SELECT
    r.movie_id,
    m.movie_title,
    ROUND(AVG(r.rating), 2) AS avg_rating,
    COUNT(*) AS num_ratings
FROM ratings r
JOIN movies m 
ON r.movie_id = m.movie_id
GROUP BY r.movie_id, m.movie_title
ORDER BY avg_rating DESC
LIMIT 10

paragraphs.text:
%md
#### 💬 Discussion — Task 2

> ##### The top 10 movies identified appear to include several older films (1990s) and the results show that all top movies have a perfect average rating of 5.0.
> ##### However, this outcome is **strongly influenced** by the number of ratings per movie. Most of these movies have very few ratings (around 1 to 3). It makes the average score statistically unreliable. A perfect score based on a small sample size does not necessarily indicate true popularity or quality. 

*To improve the result, can try incorporating a minimum threshold for number of ratings.*

msg.data:
💬 Discussion — Task 2

The top 10 movies identified appear to include several older films (1990s) and the results show that all top movies have a perfect average rating of 5.0.
However, this outcome is strongly influenced by the number of ratings per movie. Most of these movies have very few ratings (around 1 to 3). It makes the average score statistically unreliable. A perfect score based on a small sample size does not necessarily indicate true popularity or quality.

To improve the result, can try incorporating a minimum threshold for number of ratings.

paragraphs.text:
%md

#### <font color='teal'>Task 3</font> — Favourite Genre of Active Users (≥ 50 Ratings)
##### Identify users who have rated at least 50 movies and determine their favourite movie genre based on the genre they rated most frequently

---

##### The output of this query can be visualized using Apache Zeppelin’s built-in interactive chart feature.

**Chart Setting:**
* **Keys:** `genre`
* **Values:** `total_count SUM`

msg.data:
Task 3 — Favourite Genre of Active Users (≥ 50 Ratings)

Identify users who have rated at least 50 movies and determine their favourite movie genre based on the genre they rated most frequently
The output of this query can be visualized using Apache Zeppelin’s built-in interactive chart feature.

Chart Setting:

  • Keys: genre
  • Values: total_count SUM

paragraphs.text:
%sql

WITH active_users AS (
    SELECT user_id
    FROM ratings
    GROUP BY user_id
    HAVING COUNT(*) >= 50
),

base AS (
    SELECT m.*
    FROM ratings r
    JOIN active_users a ON r.user_id = a.user_id
    JOIN movies m ON r.movie_id = m.movie_id
),

exploded AS (
    SELECT
        stack(19,
            'unknown', unknown,
            'action', action,
            'adventure', adventure,
            'animation', animation,
            'childrens', childrens,
            'comedy', comedy,
            'crime', crime,
            'documentary', documentary,
            'drama', drama,
            'fantasy', fantasy,
            'film_noir', film_noir,
            'horror', horror,
            'musical', musical,
            'mystery', mystery,
            'romance', romance,
            'sci_fi', sci_fi,
            'thriller', thriller,
            'war', war,
            'western', western
        ) AS (genre, cnt)
    FROM base
)

SELECT
    genre,
    SUM(cnt) AS total_count,
    RANK() OVER (ORDER BY SUM(cnt) DESC) AS rank
FROM exploded
GROUP BY genre
ORDER BY rank

genre	total_count	rank

comedy	26880	2
action	22620	3

paragraphs.text:
%md

#### 💬 Discussion — Task 3

> ##### This analysis first identifies users who have rated at least 50 movies and count their favourite movie genre rating.

> ##### After filtering the users, their ratings were used to determine **favourite movie genre**. The results show that Drama is the most frequently rated genre, followed by Comedy and Action. Meanwhile, categories such as Documentary, Fantasy and Film-Noir receive significantly fewer ratings.

> ##### **Mainstream genres** (Drama, Comedy and Action) completely dominate the ratings, while **niche genres** (Documentary, Fantasy and Film-Noir) are underrepresented in user ratings. However, this does not necessarily reflect pure user preference because genres with more available movies are naturally more likely to be rated, introducing **structural bias** into the results.

msg.data:
💬 Discussion — Task 3

This analysis first identifies users who have rated at least 50 movies and count their favourite movie genre rating.
After filtering the users, their ratings were used to determine favourite movie genre. The results show that Drama is the most frequently rated genre, followed by Comedy and Action. Meanwhile, categories such as Documentary, Fantasy and Film-Noir receive significantly fewer ratings.
Mainstream genres (Drama, Comedy and Action) completely dominate the ratings, while niche genres (Documentary, Fantasy and Film-Noir) are underrepresented in user ratings. However, this does not necessarily reflect pure user preference because genres with more available movies are naturally more likely to be rated, introducing structural bias into the results.

%md
#### 💬 Discussion — Task 4

> ##### The filtered dataset shows that users under the age of 20 are mostly in **middle to late teenage years (15 to 19 years old)** and only one user is still under 10 years old. 
> ##### The majority of them are classified as **students** and this is expected given their age group. A small number of users are labelled as 'other' or 'none'. This is probably due to to some inconsistency or missing detail in the occupation column.

msg.data:
💬 Discussion — Task 4

The filtered dataset shows that users under the age of 20 are mostly in middle to late teenage years (15 to 19 years old) and only one user is still under 10 years old.
The majority of them are classified as students and this is expected given their age group. A small number of users are labelled as 'other' or 'none'. This is probably due to to some inconsistency or missing detail in the occupation column.

## 8. Write results to Cassandra
The processed analytical outputs from Spark are persisted into a <font color='navy'>Cassandra</font> keyspace (`movielens`) 

---

> 📋 **Tables Created**
| Table | Task | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| `avg_movie_ratings` | 1 | Average rating per movieID |
| `top10_movies` | 2 | Top 10 highest rated movies |
| `user_fav_genre` | 3 | Favourite genre per userID |
| `users_under20` | 4 | Users younger than 20 |
| `scientists_30_40` |5 | Scientists aged 30–40 |

*Note: This step demonstrates integration between Spark processing and Cassandra distributed storage for persistent analytical outputs.*

paragraphs.text:
%md

##### **Create Spark DataFrames containing the task results**

msg.data: Create Spark DataFrames containing the task results

paragraphs.text:
%pyspark

# Task 1 - Average Rating for Each Movie
avg_df = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    r.movie_id,
    m.movie_title,
    ROUND(AVG(r.rating), 2) AS avg_rating,
    COUNT(*) AS num_ratings
FROM ratings r
JOIN movies m
ON r.movie_id = m.movie_id
GROUP BY r.movie_id, m.movie_title
LIMIT 10
""")

avg_df.show(10)
avg_df.printSchema()

paragraphs.text:
%pyspark

# Task 2 - Top 10 Movies by Average Ratings
top10_df = spark.sql("""
SELECT
    r.movie_id,
    m.movie_title,
    ROUND(AVG(r.rating), 2) AS avg_rating,
    COUNT(*) AS num_ratings
FROM ratings r
JOIN movies m 
ON r.movie_id = m.movie_id
GROUP BY r.movie_id, m.movie_title
ORDER BY avg_rating DESC
LIMIT 10
""")

top10_df.show(10)
top10_df.printSchema()

paragraphs.text:
%pyspark

# Task 3 -  Favourite Genre of Active Users (≥ 50 Ratings)
genre_df = spark.sql("""
WITH active_users AS (
    SELECT user_id
    FROM ratings
    GROUP BY user_id
    HAVING COUNT(*) >= 50
),

base AS (
    SELECT m.*
    FROM ratings r
    JOIN active_users a ON r.user_id = a.user_id
    JOIN movies m ON r.movie_id = m.movie_id
),

exploded AS (
    SELECT
        stack(19,
            'unknown', unknown,
            'action', action,
            'adventure', adventure,
            'animation', animation,
            'childrens', childrens,
            'comedy', comedy,
            'crime', crime,
            'documentary', documentary,
            'drama', drama,
            'fantasy', fantasy,
            'film_noir', film_noir,
            'horror', horror,
            'musical', musical,
            'mystery', mystery,
            'romance', romance,
            'sci_fi', sci_fi,
            'thriller', thriller,
            'war', war,
            'western', western
        ) AS (genre, cnt)
    FROM base
)

SELECT
    genre,
    SUM(cnt) AS total_count,
    RANK() OVER (ORDER BY SUM(cnt) DESC) AS rank
FROM exploded
GROUP BY genre
ORDER BY rank
""")

genre_df.show()
genre_df.printSchema()

##### **Save Results into Cassandra**

msg.data: Save Results into Cassandra

paragraphs.text:
%pyspark

# Task 1 - Average Rating for Each Movie
avg_df.write \
  .format("org.apache.spark.sql.cassandra") \
  .options(table="avg_movie_ratings", keyspace="movielens") \
  .mode("append") \
  .save()
  
print("Task 1 data successfully written to Cassandra: avg_movie_ratings")

Each table is read back into a Spark DataFrame to confirm the data was written correctly

- Row count comparison between Spark DataFrames and Cassandra tables confirmed data consistency.
- Sample records retrieved from Cassandra matched the original Spark outputs.
- No missing or duplicate records were detected during validation.

9. Read back from Cassandra for validation

Each table is read back into a Spark DataFrame to confirm the data was written correctly

🎯 Results:

  • All tables were successfully created and populated in Cassandra.
  • Row count comparison between Spark DataFrames and Cassandra tables confirmed data consistency.

%pyspark

# Load Task 1 results from Cassandra table
avg_df_check = spark.read \
    .format("org.apache.spark.sql.cassandra") \
    .options(table="avg_movie_ratings", keyspace="movielens") \
    .load()

# Display results
avg_df_check.show(5)

# Print schema to verify structure matches
avg_df_check.printSchema()

# Compare row counts between Spark DataFrame and Cassandra table

print("Cassandra table count:", avg_df_check.count())
